literal: the triad, the threefold; from tri (three) — named for its triadic categories
invariant_definition: Trika ("the Triad") is the principal non-dual school of Kashmir Śaivism, named for its triadic schemata — the three goddesses Parā, Parāparā, and Aparā, and the three principles Śiva, Śakti, and the bound soul (nara / aṇu). Drawing on the Bhairava and Trika tantras and systematized by Vasugupta, Somānanda, Utpaladeva, and above all Abhinavagupta (10th–11th c.), it teaches that all reality is the self-manifestation of a single absolute consciousness (Śiva / Parama-Śiva), at once luminous (prakāśa) and self-aware (vimarśa). The world is not illusory but a free expansion of that consciousness through its autonomy (svātantrya). Its epistemic and soteriological core is the Pratyabhijñā ("recognition") philosophy: liberation is recognizing the limited self to have always been Śiva.
